QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to be notified when CPU utilization on an EC2 instance exceeds 80% for 5 consecutive minutes. Which AWS service and configuration should they use?
Explanation: Amazon CloudWatch Alarms monitor metrics and trigger actions when thresholds are breached. Configuring an alarm on the CPUUtilization metric with a threshold of 80%, evaluation period of 5 minutes, and 5 consecutive datapoints ensures the alarm only triggers during sustained high CPU usage, not brief spikes. The alarm can send notifications via SNS to email, SMS, or trigger Lambda functions.
Option1: Create a CloudWatch Alarm on CPUUtilization with a threshold of 80% and 5 evaluation periods of 1 minute each
Option2: Check CPU utilization manually in the EC2 console every 5 minutes
Option3: Enable detailed monitoring and review CloudTrail logs
Option4: Use AWS Config to monitor CPU utilization

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to collect and analyze logs from multiple EC2 instances running a web application. Which TWO services should they use? (Select TWO)
Explanation: The CloudWatch Agent installed on EC2 instances collects system-level metrics (memory, disk) and application logs, sending them to CloudWatch Logs. CloudWatch Logs Insights provides an interactive query language to search, filter, and analyze log data across multiple log groups. Together they provide centralized log collection and powerful analysis capabilities.
Option1: Install the CloudWatch Agent on EC2 instances to send logs to CloudWatch Logs
Option2: Use CloudWatch Logs Insights to query and analyze the collected logs
Option3: Download logs manually from each instance via SSH
Option4: Use AWS Config to collect application logs

QuestionText: An EC2 instance is experiencing performance issues. The SysOps administrator needs to check memory utilization but it is not visible in the default CloudWatch metrics. What should they do?
Explanation: CloudWatch does not collect memory utilization by default because it requires OS-level access. The CloudWatch Agent must be installed on the instance to collect custom metrics including memory utilization, disk usage, and swap usage. The agent is configured via a JSON configuration file that specifies which metrics to collect and at what interval.
Option1: Install and configure the CloudWatch Agent on the instance to publish memory utilization as a custom metric
Option2: Enable detailed monitoring on the EC2 instance
Option3: Check the EC2 instance metadata for memory metrics
Option4: Use AWS X-Ray to monitor memory utilization

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to automatically remediate EC2 instances that fail status checks. Which approach requires the LEAST operational overhead?
Explanation: CloudWatch Alarms can be configured with EC2 actions that automatically recover or reboot instances when status checks fail. The recover action migrates the instance to new hardware while maintaining the same instance ID, private IP, Elastic IP, and EBS attachments. This is fully automated with no custom code required—just a CloudWatch alarm with an EC2 recover action.
Option1: Create a CloudWatch Alarm on StatusCheckFailed_System that triggers an EC2 recover action
Option2: Write a Lambda function that polls instance status and reboots failed instances
Option3: Manually monitor the EC2 console and reboot instances as needed
Option4: Use AWS Systems Manager to run a reboot script on a schedule

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to track all changes to AWS resources across all accounts in their organization for compliance auditing. Which service should they configure?
Explanation: AWS CloudTrail configured as an organization trail logs all API activity across all accounts in the AWS Organization to a centralized S3 bucket. This provides a complete audit trail of who made what changes, when, and from where. CloudTrail logs include the identity of the caller, timestamp, source IP, request parameters, and response elements for every API call.
Option1: Configure AWS CloudTrail as an organization trail logging to a centralized S3 bucket
Option2: Enable VPC Flow Logs in every VPC
Option3: Use Amazon CloudWatch Events to track all changes
Option4: Enable AWS Config in each account separately

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to create a dashboard that displays key metrics from multiple AWS services in a single view. Which service should they use?
Explanation: CloudWatch Dashboards provide customizable views of AWS resources and custom metrics. You can add widgets displaying metrics from EC2, RDS, ELB, Lambda, and other services on a single dashboard. Dashboards support automatic refresh, time range selection, and can be shared across accounts. They provide a centralized operational view for monitoring application health.
Option1: Create a CloudWatch Dashboard with widgets for metrics from multiple AWS services
Option2: Use the AWS Management Console home page
Option3: Create a custom web application to display metrics
Option4: Use AWS Trusted Advisor dashboard

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to detect when an IAM policy is changed and automatically revert unauthorized changes. Which TWO services should they use together? (Select TWO)
Explanation: AWS Config rules continuously evaluate resource configurations. A custom Config rule can detect when an IAM policy is modified. AWS Systems Manager Automation can be triggered as a remediation action to automatically revert the policy to its previous state. Together they provide detection and automatic remediation of unauthorized IAM policy changes.
Option1: AWS Config with a rule to detect IAM policy changes
Option2: AWS Systems Manager Automation to automatically revert unauthorized changes
Option3: AWS CloudTrail to prevent policy changes
Option4: Amazon GuardDuty to block IAM modifications

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to monitor the health of an Application Load Balancer and its targets. Which TWO CloudWatch metrics are MOST important? (Select TWO)
Explanation: HealthyHostCount tracks the number of targets that are passing health checks—a decrease indicates targets are failing. HTTPCode_ELB_5XX_Count tracks server errors returned by the ALB itself (not the targets), indicating ALB-level issues like no healthy targets or capacity problems. Together these metrics provide visibility into both target health and ALB operational status.
Option1: HealthyHostCount to monitor the number of healthy targets
Option2: HTTPCode_ELB_5XX_Count to monitor ALB-level server errors
Option3: NetworkPacketsIn to monitor network traffic volume
Option4: CPUUtilization of the ALB

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to set up centralized logging for Lambda functions across multiple accounts. Which approach is MOST efficient?
Explanation: CloudWatch Logs subscription filters can forward log data to a centralized destination. By creating subscription filters on Lambda log groups that forward to a Kinesis Data Stream or Kinesis Data Firehose in a central logging account, logs from all accounts are aggregated. The central account can then store logs in S3 or analyze them with OpenSearch. Cross-account log delivery uses IAM roles for secure access.
Option1: Use CloudWatch Logs subscription filters to forward Lambda logs to a centralized logging account via Kinesis
Option2: SSH into each Lambda execution environment to collect logs
Option3: Store logs in DynamoDB tables in each account
Option4: Disable Lambda logging and use custom logging to S3

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to be alerted when AWS is experiencing service issues that may affect their resources. Which service provides personalized notifications?
Explanation: AWS Health Dashboard (Personal Health Dashboard) provides personalized alerts and remediation guidance when AWS is experiencing events that may impact your specific resources. Unlike the Service Health Dashboard (which shows general AWS service status), the Personal Health Dashboard filters events relevant to your account and resources, providing actionable notifications.
Option1: AWS Health Dashboard (Personal Health Dashboard) for personalized service event notifications
Option2: AWS Service Health Dashboard for general service status
Option3: Amazon CloudWatch for AWS service health
Option4: AWS Trusted Advisor for service health checks

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to monitor custom application metrics from EC2 instances. The metrics should be available in CloudWatch for alarming and dashboarding. Which approach should they use?
Explanation: The CloudWatch Agent supports custom metrics collection through StatsD and collectd protocols, or through the PutMetricData API. The agent can be configured to collect application-specific metrics and publish them to CloudWatch as custom metrics. These custom metrics can then be used in CloudWatch Alarms, Dashboards, and Logs Insights queries just like built-in AWS metrics.
Option1: Configure the CloudWatch Agent to collect and publish custom application metrics to CloudWatch
Option2: Write application logs to a local file and parse them manually
Option3: Use AWS X-Ray for custom metric collection
Option4: Store metrics in an RDS database and query them directly

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to identify which AWS API calls are being made by a specific IAM user over the past 30 days. Which service should they query?
Explanation: CloudTrail Event History provides a searchable, filterable view of the last 90 days of management events in your account. You can filter by user name, event name, resource type, and other attributes. For the specific requirement of finding API calls by a user, filtering CloudTrail Event History by the IAM user name returns all API activity for that user.
Option1: Query AWS CloudTrail Event History filtered by the IAM user name
Option2: Check the IAM user's access advisor for API calls
Option3: Review CloudWatch Logs for IAM activity
Option4: Use AWS Config to track user API calls

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to create a composite alarm that triggers only when BOTH CPU utilization is high AND memory utilization is high on an EC2 instance. Which CloudWatch feature should they use?
Explanation: CloudWatch Composite Alarms combine multiple alarms using AND, OR, and NOT logic. By creating individual alarms for CPU and memory, then a composite alarm that requires BOTH to be in ALARM state, the administrator avoids false alerts from single-metric spikes. This reduces alarm noise and ensures notifications only fire when there is a genuine resource constraint.
Option1: Create a CloudWatch Composite Alarm that requires both the CPU and memory alarms to be in ALARM state
Option2: Create two separate alarms and manually correlate them
Option3: Use a single alarm with a math expression
Option4: Configure SNS to deduplicate alarm notifications

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to automatically stop EC2 development instances that have been idle (CPU below 5%) for more than 2 hours to save costs. Which approach should they use?
Explanation: A CloudWatch Alarm on CPUUtilization with a threshold of 5%, evaluation period of 2 hours (24 datapoints at 5-minute intervals), and an EC2 stop action automatically stops idle instances. This is a built-in CloudWatch feature requiring no custom code. The alarm transitions to ALARM state when CPU stays below 5% for 2 hours and executes the stop action.
Option1: Create a CloudWatch Alarm that triggers an EC2 stop action when CPUUtilization is below 5% for 2 hours
Option2: Write a Lambda function that checks CPU every hour and stops idle instances
Option3: Use AWS Instance Scheduler to stop all instances at a fixed time
Option4: Manually check instance utilization daily and stop idle ones

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to ensure that CloudWatch Logs data is retained for exactly 1 year for compliance and then automatically deleted. Which configuration should they apply?
Explanation: CloudWatch Logs retention settings control how long log data is stored. By setting the retention period to 365 days on each log group, logs older than 1 year are automatically deleted. The default retention is indefinite (never expire), so this must be explicitly configured. Retention can be set via the console, CLI, or CloudFormation for each log group.
Option1: Set the CloudWatch Logs retention period to 365 days on the log groups
Option2: Create a Lambda function to delete old logs manually
Option3: Export logs to S3 and delete the log groups
Option4: Use S3 lifecycle policies on CloudWatch Logs

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to ensure that an RDS database can survive the failure of an Availability Zone with minimal downtime. Which configuration should they enable?
Explanation: RDS Multi-AZ deployment maintains a synchronous standby replica in a different AZ. If the primary instance fails, RDS automatically fails over to the standby within 60-120 seconds. The DNS endpoint remains the same, so applications reconnect automatically. Multi-AZ provides high availability for production databases and is recommended for any workload that cannot tolerate extended downtime.
Option1: Enable RDS Multi-AZ deployment
Option2: Create a read replica in the same AZ
Option3: Take manual snapshots every hour
Option4: Increase the RDS instance size for better reliability

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to implement automated backups for EC2 instances with EBS volumes. Backups should be retained for 30 days and taken daily. Which service should they use?
Explanation: Amazon Data Lifecycle Manager (DLM) automates the creation, retention, and deletion of EBS snapshots and EBS-backed AMIs. You create a lifecycle policy that specifies the target resources (by tag), schedule (daily), and retention period (30 days). DLM handles everything automatically—no custom scripts or Lambda functions needed.
Option1: Configure Amazon Data Lifecycle Manager with a daily snapshot policy and 30-day retention
Option2: Write a Lambda function triggered by CloudWatch Events to create snapshots
Option3: Manually create snapshots from the EC2 console daily
Option4: Use AWS Backup with a custom script

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to implement a disaster recovery solution for their application with an RTO of 4 hours and RPO of 1 hour. Which DR strategy is MOST cost-effective while meeting these requirements?
Explanation: Pilot Light maintains the minimum core infrastructure (like database replication) in the DR Region. During a disaster, additional resources (EC2 instances, load balancers) are provisioned and scaled up. With continuous database replication (RPO near zero) and automated provisioning scripts (RTO of 1-4 hours), Pilot Light meets the requirements at lower cost than Warm Standby or Active-Active.
Option1: Implement a Pilot Light strategy with database replication and automated provisioning scripts
Option2: Implement Multi-Site Active-Active across two Regions
Option3: Implement Backup and Restore with daily S3 cross-region copies
Option4: Implement Warm Standby with a full-scale environment in the DR Region

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to ensure that an Auto Scaling group can launch instances even if one AZ becomes unavailable. Which TWO configurations are important? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Configuring the Auto Scaling group across multiple AZs ensures that if one AZ fails, instances can be launched in the remaining AZs. Setting the minimum capacity to at least 2 (spread across AZs) ensures that losing one AZ still leaves at least one running instance. The AZRebalance process automatically redistributes instances across AZs when capacity becomes unbalanced.
Option1: Configure the Auto Scaling group to span multiple Availability Zones
Option2: Set the minimum capacity to ensure instances exist in multiple AZs
Option3: Use a single AZ with larger instance types
Option4: Disable the AZRebalance process

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to restore an RDS database to a specific point in time 2 hours ago after an accidental data deletion. Which feature should they use?
Explanation: RDS Point-in-Time Recovery (PITR) allows you to restore a database to any second within the backup retention period. RDS continuously backs up transaction logs to S3, enabling restoration to any point. The restore creates a new RDS instance with the data as it existed at the specified time. The administrator then redirects the application to the new instance.
Option1: Use RDS Point-in-Time Recovery to restore the database to the state 2 hours ago
Option2: Restore from the most recent automated snapshot
Option3: Use the RDS read replica as the recovery source
Option4: Query the RDS event log to find and reverse the deletion

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to implement cross-Region replication for S3 buckets containing critical business data. Which TWO prerequisites must be met? (Select TWO)
Explanation: S3 Cross-Region Replication (CRR) requires versioning to be enabled on both the source and destination buckets. An IAM role with permissions to read from the source bucket and write to the destination bucket must be configured. CRR replicates new objects automatically after the rule is created. Existing objects can be replicated using S3 Batch Replication.
Option1: Enable versioning on both the source and destination S3 buckets
Option2: Configure an IAM role with permissions for cross-Region replication
Option3: Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration on both buckets
Option4: Create a VPC endpoint for S3 in both Regions

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to test their disaster recovery plan without affecting the production environment. Which approach should they use?
Explanation: AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery (DRS) and CloudFormation enable DR testing by launching a full copy of the production environment in the DR Region using the replicated data. The test environment is isolated from production—it uses separate networking and does not affect live traffic. After testing, the environment is torn down. This validates the DR plan without any production impact.
Option1: Launch a DR test environment using replicated data in an isolated network configuration
Option2: Perform a failover of the production environment to test DR
Option3: Review the DR documentation without actually testing
Option4: Shut down production and verify the DR environment handles traffic

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to ensure that their EBS volumes are protected against accidental deletion. Which feature should they enable?
Explanation: EBS snapshots provide point-in-time backups, but to prevent accidental volume deletion, you should enable termination protection on EC2 instances (which prevents the instance and its volumes from being terminated) and set the DeleteOnTermination attribute to false on EBS volumes. Additionally, using AWS Backup with a backup plan provides automated, policy-based protection.
Option1: Set the DeleteOnTermination attribute to false on EBS volumes and enable EC2 termination protection
Option2: Increase the EBS volume size to prevent deletion
Option3: Enable EBS encryption to prevent deletion
Option4: Use instance store volumes instead of EBS

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to implement a backup strategy for DynamoDB tables that allows restoration to any point within the last 35 days. Which feature should they enable?
Explanation: DynamoDB Point-in-Time Recovery (PITR) provides continuous backups of DynamoDB table data. When enabled, you can restore the table to any point in time within the last 35 days (down to the second). PITR works without affecting table performance or availability. The restore creates a new table with the data as it existed at the specified time.
Option1: Enable DynamoDB Point-in-Time Recovery (PITR) on the table
Option2: Create manual DynamoDB backups daily
Option3: Use DynamoDB Streams to maintain a backup table
Option4: Export the table to S3 on a schedule

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to implement a centralized backup solution that manages backups across multiple AWS services (EC2 EBS RDS DynamoDB EFS). Which service should they use?
Explanation: AWS Backup provides a centralized, fully managed backup service that works across EC2, EBS, RDS, DynamoDB, EFS, FSx, and other AWS services. You create backup plans that define backup frequency, retention period, and lifecycle rules. Backup vaults store the backups with encryption. AWS Backup supports cross-Region and cross-account backup for disaster recovery.
Option1: Use AWS Backup with a backup plan covering all required services
Option2: Create separate backup scripts for each service
Option3: Use Amazon Data Lifecycle Manager for all services
Option4: Manually create backups from each service console

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to ensure that an Auto Scaling group replaces unhealthy instances detected by the load balancer. Which health check type should they configure?
Explanation: By default, Auto Scaling groups use EC2 status checks which only detect hardware/hypervisor failures. Configuring ELB health checks on the Auto Scaling group means it uses the load balancer's application-level health checks. If an instance fails the ELB health check (e.g., returns HTTP 500), the Auto Scaling group terminates it and launches a replacement.
Option1: Configure the Auto Scaling group to use ELB health checks
Option2: Increase the frequency of EC2 status checks
Option3: Create a custom health check script on each instance
Option4: Use CloudWatch alarms to detect unhealthy instances

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to implement a solution where Route 53 automatically routes traffic away from an unhealthy Region. Which TWO configurations are required? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Route 53 health checks monitor endpoint health by sending requests at regular intervals. Failover routing policy uses health check results to route traffic to a primary endpoint when healthy, and to a secondary endpoint when the primary fails. Together they provide automatic DNS-level failover between Regions when the primary Region becomes unhealthy.
Option1: Configure Route 53 health checks on the primary Region endpoint
Option2: Configure a Route 53 failover routing policy with primary and secondary records
Option3: Use Route 53 simple routing with multiple values
Option4: Configure CloudFront to handle failover

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to deploy a standardized set of AWS resources across multiple accounts in their organization. Which service should they use?
Explanation: AWS CloudFormation StackSets extend CloudFormation by enabling you to create, update, or delete stacks across multiple accounts and Regions with a single operation. StackSets use an administrator account to define the template and target accounts/Regions. This ensures consistent resource deployment and configuration across the entire organization.
Option1: Use AWS CloudFormation StackSets to deploy templates across multiple accounts and Regions
Option2: Manually create CloudFormation stacks in each account
Option3: Use AWS Config to deploy resources
Option4: Copy AMIs to each account and launch instances manually

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to patch the operating systems on a fleet of 200 EC2 instances with minimal downtime. Which AWS service automates this process?
Explanation: AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager automates the process of patching managed instances with security-related and other types of updates. You define patch baselines (which patches to approve), maintenance windows (when to apply patches), and patch groups (which instances to patch). Patch Manager handles scanning for missing patches, downloading, and installing them across the fleet.
Option1: Use AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager with maintenance windows and patch baselines
Option2: SSH into each instance and run updates manually
Option3: Create new AMIs with patches and replace all instances
Option4: Use AWS Config to apply patches

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to run a shell script on multiple EC2 instances simultaneously without SSH access. Which AWS service and feature should they use?
Explanation: AWS Systems Manager Run Command allows you to remotely execute commands on managed instances without SSH or RDP access. You select target instances (by tag, instance ID, or resource group), choose a command document (like AWS-RunShellScript), and specify the commands. Run Command logs output to S3 or CloudWatch Logs and provides execution status for each instance.
Option1: Use AWS Systems Manager Run Command with the AWS-RunShellScript document
Option2: Use AWS Lambda to SSH into each instance
Option3: Use EC2 User Data to run the script
Option4: Use AWS CodeDeploy to execute the script

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to create a golden AMI that includes specific software packages and security configurations. The AMI should be automatically rebuilt monthly. Which service should they use?
Explanation: EC2 Image Builder automates the creation, testing, and distribution of AMIs. You define an image pipeline with a base AMI, build components (software installation, configuration), test components (validation), and a distribution configuration. Scheduling the pipeline to run monthly ensures the golden AMI is regularly updated with the latest patches and configurations.
Option1: Use EC2 Image Builder with a monthly scheduled pipeline
Option2: Manually launch an instance and create an AMI each month
Option3: Use AWS Lambda to create AMIs on a schedule
Option4: Use CloudFormation to define the AMI

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to ensure that all EC2 instances in their account have the Systems Manager Agent installed and are registered as managed instances. Which TWO steps are required? (Select TWO)
Explanation: The SSM Agent must be installed on EC2 instances (it is pre-installed on Amazon Linux 2, Amazon Linux 2023, and some other AMIs). The instance must have an IAM instance profile with the AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore policy, which grants permissions for the agent to communicate with the Systems Manager service. Without both, the instance cannot be managed by Systems Manager.
Option1: Ensure the SSM Agent is installed and running on all EC2 instances
Option2: Attach an IAM instance profile with the AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore policy
Option3: Open port 22 in all security groups
Option4: Install the CloudWatch Agent on all instances

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to manage configuration parameters for their application across multiple environments (dev staging prod). Which AWS service provides hierarchical parameter storage?
Explanation: AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store provides secure, hierarchical storage for configuration data and secrets. Parameters can be organized in a hierarchy (e.g., /app/dev/db-host, /app/prod/db-host) making it easy to manage environment-specific configurations. Parameter Store supports String, StringList, and SecureString (KMS-encrypted) types and integrates with IAM for access control.
Option1: Use AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store with a hierarchical naming convention
Option2: Store parameters in environment variables on each instance
Option3: Use a shared S3 bucket with configuration files
Option4: Hardcode parameters in the application

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to automate the response to a specific CloudWatch Alarm by executing a series of remediation steps. Which TWO services should they use together? (Select TWO)
Explanation: CloudWatch Alarms can trigger SNS notifications, which can invoke Lambda functions or Systems Manager Automation documents. Systems Manager Automation provides pre-built and custom runbooks that execute multi-step remediation workflows (like restarting services, scaling resources, or creating snapshots). Together they provide automated incident response triggered by metric thresholds.
Option1: Amazon CloudWatch Alarms to detect the condition and trigger an action
Option2: AWS Systems Manager Automation to execute the remediation runbook
Option3: AWS Config to remediate the alarm
Option4: AWS Trusted Advisor to fix the issue

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to deploy an application update to EC2 instances in an Auto Scaling group with zero downtime. Which deployment strategy should they use?
Explanation: A rolling update with CloudFormation UpdatePolicy replaces instances in batches. Setting MinInstancesInService ensures a minimum number of healthy instances are always running during the update. Each batch of old instances is terminated and replaced with new instances running the updated AMI or configuration. This provides zero downtime as the application remains available throughout the update.
Option1: Use CloudFormation with a rolling UpdatePolicy and MinInstancesInService configured
Option2: Terminate all instances and let Auto Scaling launch new ones
Option3: Manually update each instance one at a time
Option4: Create a new Auto Scaling group and delete the old one immediately

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to ensure that CloudFormation stacks cannot be accidentally deleted by junior administrators. Which feature should they enable?
Explanation: CloudFormation termination protection prevents a stack from being accidentally deleted. When enabled, any attempt to delete the stack is rejected until termination protection is explicitly disabled. This is a critical safeguard for production stacks containing important resources. It can be enabled during stack creation or updated on existing stacks.
Option1: Enable CloudFormation termination protection on the stack
Option2: Remove delete permissions from all IAM users
Option3: Use a DeletionPolicy on every resource
Option4: Create a CloudWatch Alarm for stack deletion events

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to inventory all software installed on their EC2 fleet and track changes over time. Which Systems Manager feature provides this capability?
Explanation: Systems Manager Inventory collects metadata about your managed instances including installed applications, OS details, network configuration, Windows updates, and custom inventory types. Inventory data is collected periodically and stored in Systems Manager. Combined with AWS Config, you can track changes to inventory over time and set up compliance rules.
Option1: Use AWS Systems Manager Inventory to collect and track software inventory across the fleet
Option2: SSH into each instance and run package listing commands
Option3: Use AWS Config rules to check installed software
Option4: Use Amazon Inspector to inventory software

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to define the desired state of EC2 instances (specific packages installed specific services running) and automatically remediate drift. Which Systems Manager feature should they use?
Explanation: Systems Manager State Manager creates associations that define the desired state of managed instances. Associations can specify that certain packages must be installed, services must be running, or scripts must be executed at regular intervals. If an instance drifts from the desired state, State Manager automatically re-applies the configuration to bring it back into compliance.
Option1: Use AWS Systems Manager State Manager with associations defining the desired state
Option2: Write a cron job on each instance to check configuration
Option3: Use AWS Config rules to enforce instance state
Option4: Manually check and fix instances weekly

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to create a CloudFormation template that provisions different resource configurations based on the AWS Region where the stack is deployed. Which template feature should they use?
Explanation: CloudFormation Mappings define a set of key-value pairs that you can use to specify conditional parameter values. Combined with the Fn::FindInMap intrinsic function and the AWS::Region pseudo parameter, you can create Region-specific configurations (like AMI IDs, instance types, or CIDR ranges) in a single template that works across multiple Regions.
Option1: Use CloudFormation Mappings with Fn::FindInMap and the AWS::Region pseudo parameter
Option2: Create separate templates for each Region
Option3: Use CloudFormation Parameters with default values
Option4: Hardcode Region-specific values in the template

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to ensure that all S3 buckets in their account have encryption enabled. Which AWS service continuously monitors this compliance requirement?
Explanation: AWS Config with the s3-bucket-server-side-encryption-enabled managed rule continuously evaluates all S3 buckets and flags any bucket without server-side encryption as non-compliant. Config provides a compliance dashboard showing the status of all evaluated resources. Automatic remediation can be configured to enable encryption on non-compliant buckets.
Option1: Use AWS Config with the s3-bucket-server-side-encryption-enabled managed rule
Option2: Manually check each bucket's encryption settings monthly
Option3: Use AWS Trusted Advisor to check encryption
Option4: Enable CloudTrail to detect unencrypted buckets

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to rotate IAM access keys for all users in the account. Keys older than 90 days should be identified and disabled. Which TWO approaches should they use? (Select TWO)
Explanation: AWS Config with the access-keys-rotated managed rule identifies IAM users with access keys older than the specified maximum age (90 days). The IAM Credential Report provides a downloadable CSV with details about all IAM users' credentials including access key age, last used date, and MFA status. Together they provide detection and reporting for key rotation compliance.
Option1: Use AWS Config with the access-keys-rotated rule to identify non-compliant keys
Option2: Generate an IAM Credential Report to review access key ages
Option3: Delete all IAM users and recreate them with new keys
Option4: Use CloudTrail to find old access keys

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to enforce MFA for all IAM users when accessing the AWS Management Console. Which approach should they implement?
Explanation: An IAM policy with a Deny statement and a condition that checks for the absence of MFA (aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent: false) denies all actions except those needed to set up MFA. This forces users to authenticate with MFA before they can perform any actions. The policy is attached to an IAM group that all users belong to, ensuring universal enforcement.
Option1: Create an IAM policy that denies all actions unless MFA is present and attach it to all users
Option2: Enable MFA on the root account only
Option3: Use AWS Organizations SCP to require MFA
Option4: Send email reminders to users about MFA

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator discovers that an EC2 instance may have been compromised. Which TWO immediate actions should they take? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Isolating the instance by changing its security group to one that denies all inbound and outbound traffic prevents the attacker from communicating with the instance or exfiltrating data while preserving evidence. Creating an EBS snapshot captures the current state of the disk for forensic analysis. These actions contain the threat and preserve evidence without destroying the instance.
Option1: Change the instance's security group to deny all inbound and outbound traffic
Option2: Create EBS snapshots of the instance's volumes for forensic analysis
Option3: Immediately terminate the instance to stop the attack
Option4: Reboot the instance to clear any malware

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to ensure that EC2 instances are only launched in specific VPC subnets. Which IAM policy condition should they use?
Explanation: The ec2:Subnet condition key in an IAM policy restricts which subnets can be specified when launching EC2 instances. By creating a policy with a Deny statement on ec2:RunInstances where the subnet is NOT in the approved list, administrators prevent instances from being launched in unauthorized subnets. This enforces network segmentation at the IAM level.
Option1: Use an IAM policy with the ec2:Subnet condition key to restrict instance launches to approved subnets
Option2: Remove all subnets except the approved ones from the VPC
Option3: Use security groups to restrict instance placement
Option4: Configure NACLs to prevent instance launches

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to detect when security groups are modified to allow unrestricted SSH access (0.0.0.0/0 on port 22). Which service provides this detection?
Explanation: AWS Config with the restricted-ssh managed rule continuously evaluates security groups and flags any that allow unrestricted inbound SSH access (0.0.0.0/0 or ::/0 on port 22) as non-compliant. Config can trigger automatic remediation via Systems Manager Automation to remove the offending rule. This provides both detection and optional automated remediation.
Option1: Use AWS Config with the restricted-ssh managed rule
Option2: Review security groups manually each week
Option3: Use VPC Flow Logs to detect SSH access
Option4: Use Amazon GuardDuty to monitor security group changes

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to implement a solution where all data written to EBS volumes is automatically encrypted. Which approach enforces this at the account level?
Explanation: The EBS encryption by default setting, when enabled at the account level in a specific Region, ensures that all new EBS volumes and snapshots created in that Region are automatically encrypted using the specified default KMS key. This eliminates the need for users to remember to enable encryption on each volume and provides consistent encryption enforcement.
Option1: Enable EBS encryption by default at the account level in each Region
Option2: Create an IAM policy that requires encryption on every volume
Option3: Use AWS Config to detect unencrypted volumes after creation
Option4: Encrypt volumes manually after they are created

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to audit their AWS environment against CIS (Center for Internet Security) benchmarks. Which TWO services help with this? (Select TWO)
Explanation: AWS Security Hub provides automated compliance checks against CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark, running continuous evaluations of your AWS resources. AWS Config conformance packs provide pre-built collections of Config rules mapped to compliance frameworks including CIS benchmarks. Together they provide comprehensive compliance auditing with dashboards and detailed findings.
Option1: AWS Security Hub with CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark checks
Option2: AWS Config conformance packs for CIS compliance rules
Option3: AWS Trusted Advisor security checks
Option4: Amazon Inspector for CIS benchmarking

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to ensure that CloudTrail log files have not been tampered with after delivery. Which CloudTrail feature provides this integrity validation?
Explanation: CloudTrail log file integrity validation creates a digitally signed digest file for each hour of log delivery. The digest file contains hashes of the log files delivered during that hour. Using the aws cloudtrail validate-logs CLI command, you can verify that log files have not been modified, deleted, or forged after CloudTrail delivered them. This is critical for forensic and compliance purposes.
Option1: Enable CloudTrail log file integrity validation
Option2: Enable CloudTrail encryption with KMS
Option3: Store CloudTrail logs in a versioned S3 bucket
Option4: Enable CloudTrail Insights

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to grant temporary elevated access to a developer for troubleshooting a production issue. The access should automatically expire after 4 hours. Which approach should they use?
Explanation: An IAM role with a session duration of 4 hours provides temporary elevated access. The developer assumes the role using STS AssumeRole, receiving temporary credentials that expire after 4 hours. The role's trust policy restricts who can assume it, and the permissions policy grants only the access needed for troubleshooting. No permanent credential changes are needed.
Option1: Create an IAM role with the required permissions and a 4-hour maximum session duration
Option2: Create a temporary IAM user and delete it after 4 hours
Option3: Share the administrator's access keys for 4 hours
Option4: Add the developer to an admin group and remove them later

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to prevent any user from disabling CloudTrail logging in any account within their AWS Organization. Which feature should they use?
Explanation: An SCP (Service Control Policy) in AWS Organizations with a Deny statement on cloudtrail:StopLogging and cloudtrail:DeleteTrail prevents any user or role in member accounts from disabling or deleting CloudTrail trails. SCPs are the highest-priority permission boundary—even account administrators cannot override them. This ensures continuous audit logging across the organization.
Option1: Apply an SCP that denies cloudtrail:StopLogging and cloudtrail:DeleteTrail actions
Option2: Enable CloudTrail in each account and hope no one disables it
Option3: Use IAM policies in each account to prevent trail deletion
Option4: Configure CloudWatch Alarms for trail status changes

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to ensure that all S3 buckets in their account have encryption enabled. Which AWS service continuously monitors this compliance requirement?
Explanation: AWS Config with the s3-bucket-server-side-encryption-enabled managed rule continuously evaluates all S3 buckets and flags any bucket without server-side encryption as non-compliant. Config provides a compliance dashboard showing the status of all evaluated resources. Automatic remediation can be configured to enable encryption on non-compliant buckets.
Option1: Use AWS Config with the s3-bucket-server-side-encryption-enabled managed rule
Option2: Manually check each bucket's encryption settings monthly
Option3: Use AWS Trusted Advisor to check encryption
Option4: Enable CloudTrail to detect unencrypted buckets

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to rotate IAM access keys for all users in the account. Keys older than 90 days should be identified and disabled. Which TWO approaches should they use? (Select TWO)
Explanation: AWS Config with the access-keys-rotated managed rule identifies IAM users with access keys older than the specified maximum age (90 days). The IAM Credential Report provides a downloadable CSV with details about all IAM users' credentials including access key age, last used date, and MFA status. Together they provide detection and reporting for key rotation compliance.
Option1: Use AWS Config with the access-keys-rotated rule to identify non-compliant keys
Option2: Generate an IAM Credential Report to review access key ages
Option3: Delete all IAM users and recreate them with new keys
Option4: Use CloudTrail to find old access keys

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to enforce MFA for all IAM users when accessing the AWS Management Console. Which approach should they implement?
Explanation: An IAM policy with a Deny statement and a condition that checks for the absence of MFA (aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent: false) denies all actions except those needed to set up MFA. This forces users to authenticate with MFA before they can perform any actions. The policy is attached to an IAM group that all users belong to, ensuring universal enforcement.
Option1: Create an IAM policy that denies all actions unless MFA is present and attach it to all users
Option2: Enable MFA on the root account only
Option3: Use AWS Organizations SCP to require MFA
Option4: Send email reminders to users about MFA

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator discovers that an EC2 instance may have been compromised. Which TWO immediate actions should they take? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Isolating the instance by changing its security group to one that denies all inbound and outbound traffic prevents the attacker from communicating with the instance or exfiltrating data while preserving evidence. Creating an EBS snapshot captures the current state of the disk for forensic analysis. These actions contain the threat and preserve evidence without destroying the instance.
Option1: Change the instance's security group to deny all inbound and outbound traffic
Option2: Create EBS snapshots of the instance's volumes for forensic analysis
Option3: Immediately terminate the instance to stop the attack
Option4: Reboot the instance to clear any malware

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to ensure that EC2 instances are only launched in specific VPC subnets. Which IAM policy condition should they use?
Explanation: The ec2:Subnet condition key in an IAM policy restricts which subnets can be specified when launching EC2 instances. By creating a policy with a Deny statement on ec2:RunInstances where the subnet is NOT in the approved list, administrators prevent instances from being launched in unauthorized subnets. This enforces network segmentation at the IAM level.
Option1: Use an IAM policy with the ec2:Subnet condition key to restrict instance launches to approved subnets
Option2: Remove all subnets except the approved ones from the VPC
Option3: Use security groups to restrict instance placement
Option4: Configure NACLs to prevent instance launches

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to detect when security groups are modified to allow unrestricted SSH access (0.0.0.0/0 on port 22). Which service provides this detection?
Explanation: AWS Config with the restricted-ssh managed rule continuously evaluates security groups and flags any that allow unrestricted inbound SSH access (0.0.0.0/0 or ::/0 on port 22) as non-compliant. Config can trigger automatic remediation via Systems Manager Automation to remove the offending rule. This provides both detection and optional automated remediation.
Option1: Use AWS Config with the restricted-ssh managed rule
Option2: Review security groups manually each week
Option3: Use VPC Flow Logs to detect SSH access
Option4: Use Amazon GuardDuty to monitor security group changes

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to implement a solution where all data written to EBS volumes is automatically encrypted. Which approach enforces this at the account level?
Explanation: The EBS encryption by default setting, when enabled at the account level in a specific Region, ensures that all new EBS volumes and snapshots created in that Region are automatically encrypted using the specified default KMS key. This eliminates the need for users to remember to enable encryption on each volume and provides consistent encryption enforcement.
Option1: Enable EBS encryption by default at the account level in each Region
Option2: Create an IAM policy that requires encryption on every volume
Option3: Use AWS Config to detect unencrypted volumes after creation
Option4: Encrypt volumes manually after they are created

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to audit their AWS environment against CIS (Center for Internet Security) benchmarks. Which TWO services help with this? (Select TWO)
Explanation: AWS Security Hub provides automated compliance checks against CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark, running continuous evaluations of your AWS resources. AWS Config conformance packs provide pre-built collections of Config rules mapped to compliance frameworks including CIS benchmarks. Together they provide comprehensive compliance auditing with dashboards and detailed findings.
Option1: AWS Security Hub with CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark checks
Option2: AWS Config conformance packs for CIS compliance rules
Option3: AWS Trusted Advisor security checks
Option4: Amazon Inspector for CIS benchmarking

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to ensure that CloudTrail log files have not been tampered with after delivery. Which CloudTrail feature provides this integrity validation?
Explanation: CloudTrail log file integrity validation creates a digitally signed digest file for each hour of log delivery. The digest file contains hashes of the log files delivered during that hour. Using the aws cloudtrail validate-logs CLI command, you can verify that log files have not been modified, deleted, or forged after CloudTrail delivered them. This is critical for forensic and compliance purposes.
Option1: Enable CloudTrail log file integrity validation
Option2: Enable CloudTrail encryption with KMS
Option3: Store CloudTrail logs in a versioned S3 bucket
Option4: Enable CloudTrail Insights

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to grant temporary elevated access to a developer for troubleshooting a production issue. The access should automatically expire after 4 hours. Which approach should they use?
Explanation: An IAM role with a session duration of 4 hours provides temporary elevated access. The developer assumes the role using STS AssumeRole, receiving temporary credentials that expire after 4 hours. The role's trust policy restricts who can assume it, and the permissions policy grants only the access needed for troubleshooting. No permanent credential changes are needed.
Option1: Create an IAM role with the required permissions and a 4-hour maximum session duration
Option2: Create a temporary IAM user and delete it after 4 hours
Option3: Share the administrator's access keys for 4 hours
Option4: Add the developer to an admin group and remove them later

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to prevent any user from disabling CloudTrail logging in any account within their AWS Organization. Which feature should they use?
Explanation: An SCP (Service Control Policy) in AWS Organizations with a Deny statement on cloudtrail:StopLogging and cloudtrail:DeleteTrail prevents any user or role in member accounts from disabling or deleting CloudTrail trails. SCPs are the highest-priority permission boundary—even account administrators cannot override them. This ensures continuous audit logging across the organization.
Option1: Apply an SCP that denies cloudtrail:StopLogging and cloudtrail:DeleteTrail actions
Option2: Enable CloudTrail in each account and hope no one disables it
Option3: Use IAM policies in each account to prevent trail deletion
Option4: Configure CloudWatch Alarms for trail status changes

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to troubleshoot connectivity issues between an EC2 instance in a private subnet and the internet. Which TWO components should they verify? (Select TWO)
Explanation: A NAT Gateway in a public subnet is required for instances in private subnets to access the internet. The private subnet's route table must have a route sending 0.0.0.0/0 traffic to the NAT Gateway. If either is missing or misconfigured, the instance cannot reach the internet. The NAT Gateway itself must be in a public subnet with a route to an Internet Gateway.
Option1: Verify a NAT Gateway exists in a public subnet and is operational
Option2: Check the private subnet route table has a route to the NAT Gateway for 0.0.0.0/0
Option3: Check if the instance has an Elastic IP address
Option4: Verify the instance has a public DNS name

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to allow an EC2 instance in a private subnet to resolve DNS names for AWS services. Which VPC setting must be enabled?
Explanation: The enableDnsSupport VPC attribute (set to true by default) enables the Amazon-provided DNS server at the 169.254.169.253 address. The enableDnsHostnames attribute enables DNS hostnames for instances. Both must be enabled for instances to resolve public DNS names. If DNS resolution is not working, these settings should be verified first.
Option1: Verify that enableDnsSupport and enableDnsHostnames are enabled on the VPC
Option2: Configure a custom DNS server on each instance
Option3: Create a Route 53 private hosted zone
Option4: Open port 53 in the instance's security group

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to connect two VPCs in different Regions so that instances in each VPC can communicate using private IP addresses. Which solution should they implement?
Explanation: VPC Peering supports inter-Region peering, creating a networking connection between two VPCs that enables routing traffic between them using private IP addresses. Instances in either VPC can communicate as if they are in the same network. The peering connection requires route table entries in both VPCs pointing to the peering connection for the other VPC's CIDR range.
Option1: Create an inter-Region VPC Peering connection and update route tables in both VPCs
Option2: Use a VPN connection between the two VPCs
Option3: Create an Internet Gateway in each VPC and route through the public internet
Option4: Use AWS Direct Connect between the Regions

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator is troubleshooting why an EC2 instance cannot communicate with another instance in the same VPC. Which TWO things should they check first? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Security groups control inbound and outbound traffic at the instance level. If the destination instance's security group doesn't allow inbound traffic from the source instance's IP or security group, communication is blocked. Network ACLs control traffic at the subnet level and are stateless—both inbound and outbound rules must allow the traffic. These are the two most common causes of intra-VPC connectivity issues.
Option1: Check the security groups on both instances for appropriate allow rules
Option2: Check the Network ACLs on both subnets for allow and deny rules
Option3: Verify both instances have Elastic IP addresses
Option4: Check the VPC's Internet Gateway configuration

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to configure a VPC so that instances in a public subnet can be accessed from the internet. Which TWO components must be configured? (Select TWO)
Explanation: An Internet Gateway attached to the VPC provides a target for internet-routable traffic. The public subnet's route table must have a route sending 0.0.0.0/0 traffic to the Internet Gateway. Additionally, instances need public or Elastic IP addresses, and security groups must allow the desired inbound traffic. Without both the IGW and the route, internet access is not possible.
Option1: Attach an Internet Gateway to the VPC
Option2: Add a route to 0.0.0.0/0 pointing to the Internet Gateway in the public subnet's route table
Option3: Create a NAT Gateway in the public subnet
Option4: Enable VPC Flow Logs

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to implement a solution where traffic between their VPC and S3 does not traverse the public internet. Which service should they configure?
Explanation: A VPC Gateway Endpoint for S3 creates a private connection between the VPC and S3 that stays entirely within the AWS network. Traffic never traverses the public internet. Gateway Endpoints are free (no hourly or data processing charges) and are configured by adding a route in the subnet route table pointing to the endpoint. They support S3 and DynamoDB.
Option1: Create a VPC Gateway Endpoint for S3
Option2: Use AWS Direct Connect for S3 access
Option3: Configure a NAT Gateway for S3 traffic
Option4: Use S3 Transfer Acceleration

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to distribute incoming traffic across EC2 instances in multiple Availability Zones. The application uses HTTP and requires path-based routing. Which load balancer type should they use?
Explanation: Application Load Balancer (ALB) operates at Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS) and supports advanced routing features including path-based routing (/api/* to one target group, /images/* to another), host-based routing, and query string routing. ALB distributes traffic across targets in multiple AZs and performs health checks to ensure traffic only goes to healthy instances.
Option1: Use an Application Load Balancer with path-based routing rules
Option2: Use a Network Load Balancer
Option3: Use a Classic Load Balancer
Option4: Use Amazon CloudFront

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to troubleshoot intermittent connectivity issues between an on-premises network and their AWS VPC connected via VPN. Which TWO tools should they use? (Select TWO)
Explanation: VPC Flow Logs capture information about IP traffic going to and from network interfaces in the VPC, showing accepted and rejected traffic. CloudWatch metrics for the VPN connection show tunnel status, data transfer rates, and packet counts. Together they help identify whether traffic is reaching the VPC (Flow Logs) and whether the VPN tunnels are healthy (CloudWatch metrics).
Option1: Enable VPC Flow Logs to analyze traffic patterns and rejected connections
Option2: Monitor CloudWatch metrics for VPN tunnel status and throughput
Option3: Use AWS X-Ray to trace network requests
Option4: Check the S3 bucket for VPN logs

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to configure CloudFront to serve content from an S3 bucket while preventing direct access to the S3 bucket. Which feature should they configure?
Explanation: CloudFront Origin Access Control (OAC) restricts access to the S3 bucket so that content can only be accessed through CloudFront, not directly via the S3 URL. The S3 bucket policy is updated to allow access only from the CloudFront distribution's OAC. This ensures all requests go through CloudFront, benefiting from caching, HTTPS, and WAF protection.
Option1: Configure CloudFront Origin Access Control (OAC) and update the S3 bucket policy
Option2: Make the S3 bucket public and use CloudFront as a pass-through
Option3: Use a VPC endpoint for CloudFront to S3 access
Option4: Enable S3 Block Public Access without configuring OAC

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to connect their on-premises network to multiple VPCs through a central hub. Which AWS service simplifies this network architecture?
Explanation: AWS Transit Gateway acts as a central hub connecting multiple VPCs and on-premises networks. Instead of creating individual VPN connections to each VPC, you create a single VPN connection to the Transit Gateway, which routes traffic to the appropriate VPC based on route tables. Transit Gateway supports transitive routing, simplifying complex network topologies.
Option1: Use AWS Transit Gateway as a central hub for VPC and on-premises connectivity
Option2: Create individual VPN connections to each VPC
Option3: Use VPC Peering between all VPCs and the on-premises network
Option4: Use AWS Direct Connect to each VPC separately

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to implement DNS resolution for resources in a private VPC that are not accessible from the public internet. Which Route 53 feature should they use?
Explanation: Route 53 Private Hosted Zones allow you to route traffic within one or more VPCs without exposing resources to the public internet. You create a private hosted zone, associate it with VPCs, and create DNS records for your private resources. Instances in the associated VPCs can resolve these DNS names, but they are not accessible from outside the VPCs.
Option1: Create a Route 53 Private Hosted Zone associated with the VPC
Option2: Create a public hosted zone with restricted access
Option3: Configure custom DNS servers on each instance
Option4: Use VPC DHCP options with a third-party DNS

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to ensure that all traffic between their VPC and AWS services uses private IP addresses and does not traverse the internet. Which TWO types of VPC endpoints should they consider? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Gateway Endpoints provide private connectivity to S3 and DynamoDB through route table entries—they are free and highly available. Interface Endpoints (powered by AWS PrivateLink) provide private connectivity to most other AWS services using elastic network interfaces with private IPs in your subnet. Together they cover the full range of AWS services with private connectivity.
Option1: VPC Gateway Endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB
Option2: VPC Interface Endpoints (AWS PrivateLink) for other AWS services
Option3: NAT Gateway for private AWS service access
Option4: Internet Gateway with restrictive security groups

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to troubleshoot why a new security group rule allowing HTTPS traffic is not working. The instance is in a public subnet with an Internet Gateway. What should they check?
Explanation: Security groups are stateful (return traffic is automatically allowed), but Network ACLs are stateless—both inbound and outbound rules must explicitly allow the traffic. If the NACL on the subnet has a deny rule for HTTPS (port 443) or the ephemeral port range, traffic will be blocked even if the security group allows it. NACLs are evaluated before security groups.
Option1: Check the Network ACL on the subnet for deny rules that may be blocking HTTPS or return traffic on ephemeral ports
Option2: Restart the EC2 instance to apply the security group change
Option3: Detach and reattach the Internet Gateway
Option4: Enable VPC Flow Logs and wait 24 hours

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to identify EC2 instances that are consistently underutilized and recommend right-sizing. Which TWO tools should they use? (Select TWO)
Explanation: AWS Compute Optimizer analyzes CloudWatch metrics (CPU, memory, network, disk) and provides specific right-sizing recommendations with projected cost savings and performance impact. AWS Cost Explorer provides usage and cost data that helps identify spending patterns and underutilized resources. Together they provide both technical recommendations and financial analysis for optimization.
Option1: AWS Compute Optimizer for right-sizing recommendations
Option2: AWS Cost Explorer for usage and cost analysis
Option3: AWS Trusted Advisor for all optimization needs
Option4: Amazon Inspector for performance analysis

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator notices that their S3 storage costs are increasing rapidly. Most objects are accessed frequently for the first week then rarely accessed. Which S3 feature should they configure to reduce costs?
Explanation: S3 Lifecycle policies automatically transition objects between storage classes based on age. Configuring a rule to move objects to S3 Standard-IA after 30 days and to S3 Glacier after 90 days significantly reduces storage costs for aging data. Standard-IA costs ~40% less than Standard, and Glacier costs ~80% less, while still providing access when needed.
Option1: Configure S3 Lifecycle policies to transition objects to cheaper storage classes based on age
Option2: Delete all objects older than 7 days
Option3: Move all objects to Glacier immediately
Option4: Enable S3 Intelligent-Tiering for all objects

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to reduce the cost of running a development environment that is only used during business hours (8 AM to 6 PM weekdays). Which approach is MOST effective?
Explanation: AWS Instance Scheduler (a solution that uses Lambda and DynamoDB) automatically starts and stops EC2 and RDS instances based on a defined schedule. Stopping instances outside business hours (evenings and weekends) reduces running time from 168 hours/week to ~50 hours/week, saving approximately 70% on compute costs for the development environment.
Option1: Use AWS Instance Scheduler to stop instances outside business hours
Option2: Switch all instances to Spot Instances
Option3: Use Reserved Instances for the development environment
Option4: Reduce instance sizes during off-hours

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to optimize the performance of an RDS MySQL database that is experiencing slow read queries. Which TWO approaches should they consider? (Select TWO)
Explanation: RDS Read Replicas offload read traffic from the primary instance, distributing the query load. The application directs read queries to the replica endpoint while writes go to the primary. Amazon ElastiCache stores frequently queried results in memory, eliminating database reads entirely for cached data. Together they significantly reduce primary database load and improve read performance.
Option1: Create RDS Read Replicas to distribute read traffic
Option2: Implement Amazon ElastiCache to cache frequently queried data
Option3: Increase the RDS instance storage size
Option4: Enable Multi-AZ for read performance

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to identify unused Elastic IP addresses and unattached EBS volumes that are incurring unnecessary charges. Which AWS service provides these recommendations?
Explanation: AWS Trusted Advisor includes cost optimization checks that identify idle and underutilized resources including unassociated Elastic IP addresses (charged when not attached to a running instance), underutilized EBS volumes, idle load balancers, and unused Reserved Instances. These checks are available with Business or Enterprise Support plans.
Option1: Use AWS Trusted Advisor cost optimization checks
Option2: Review the AWS billing dashboard manually
Option3: Use Amazon CloudWatch to find unused resources
Option4: Use AWS Config to identify unused resources

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to reduce data transfer costs between EC2 instances and S3 within the same Region. Which solution eliminates data transfer charges?
Explanation: A VPC Gateway Endpoint for S3 routes traffic between the VPC and S3 over the AWS private network. There are no data processing charges for Gateway Endpoints (unlike NAT Gateways which charge per GB). Data transfer between EC2 and S3 within the same Region through a Gateway Endpoint is free, eliminating the data transfer costs that would occur through a NAT Gateway.
Option1: Create a VPC Gateway Endpoint for S3 to eliminate NAT Gateway data processing charges
Option2: Use S3 Transfer Acceleration
Option3: Move EC2 instances to a public subnet
Option4: Enable S3 Cross-Region Replication

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to optimize the performance of an Auto Scaling group that is slow to respond to traffic spikes. Instances take 5 minutes to become healthy after launch. Which TWO configurations should they adjust? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Predictive scaling uses machine learning to analyze historical traffic patterns and proactively scales out before anticipated demand increases, ensuring capacity is ready before traffic arrives. Warm pools maintain pre-initialized instances in a stopped or running state, reducing the time to add capacity from minutes (full launch + initialization) to seconds (just start the instance).
Option1: Enable predictive scaling based on historical traffic patterns
Option2: Configure a warm pool to maintain pre-initialized instances
Option3: Increase the cooldown period to prevent rapid scaling
Option4: Use larger instance types instead of scaling out

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to choose the most cost-effective EBS volume type for a workload that requires high throughput for sequential reads but does not need high IOPS. Which volume type should they select?
Explanation: Throughput Optimized HDD (st1) is designed for frequently accessed, throughput-intensive workloads like big data, data warehouses, and log processing. It provides up to 500 MiB/s throughput at a significantly lower cost per GB than SSD volumes. For sequential read workloads that prioritize throughput over IOPS, st1 provides the best price-performance ratio.
Option1: Throughput Optimized HDD (st1)
Option2: Provisioned IOPS SSD (io2)
Option3: General Purpose SSD (gp3)
Option4: Cold HDD (sc1)

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator wants to receive alerts when their AWS spending exceeds a defined threshold. Which TWO services should they configure? (Select TWO)
Explanation: AWS Budgets allows you to set custom cost and usage budgets with configurable alert thresholds. When actual or forecasted spending exceeds the threshold, Budgets sends notifications via SNS (email, SMS). CloudWatch Billing Alarms monitor the EstimatedCharges metric and trigger when spending exceeds a defined amount. Together they provide comprehensive cost alerting.
Option1: Configure AWS Budgets with cost thresholds and SNS notifications
Option2: Set up CloudWatch Billing Alarms on the EstimatedCharges metric
Option3: Use AWS Cost Explorer to set spending limits
Option4: Configure AWS Trusted Advisor cost alerts

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to detect anomalous API activity in their AWS account such as unusual spikes in API calls or calls from unexpected Regions. Which service provides this capability?
Explanation: CloudTrail Insights automatically analyzes CloudTrail management events to detect unusual API activity. It establishes a baseline of normal API call patterns and alerts when activity deviates significantly—such as spikes in resource provisioning, bursts of IAM actions, or API calls from unusual Regions. Insights events are logged separately and can trigger EventBridge rules for automated response.
Option1: Enable AWS CloudTrail Insights to detect anomalous API activity
Option2: Manually review CloudTrail logs daily for anomalies
Option3: Use Amazon GuardDuty for API call analysis
Option4: Configure CloudWatch Alarms on every API call metric

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to monitor the replication lag of an RDS read replica. If the lag exceeds 30 seconds they need to be alerted. Which CloudWatch metric should they monitor?
Explanation: The ReplicaLag CloudWatch metric for RDS measures the time (in seconds) that the read replica is behind the primary instance. Creating a CloudWatch Alarm on this metric with a threshold of 30 seconds alerts the administrator when replication falls behind. High replica lag can cause stale reads and may indicate the replica is undersized or the primary has high write throughput.
Option1: Create a CloudWatch Alarm on the RDS ReplicaLag metric with a threshold of 30 seconds
Option2: Monitor the CPUUtilization metric on the read replica
Option3: Check the DatabaseConnections metric
Option4: Monitor the FreeStorageSpace metric

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to aggregate CloudWatch metrics from multiple accounts into a single monitoring account. Which CloudWatch feature enables this?
Explanation: CloudWatch cross-account observability allows you to monitor and troubleshoot applications that span multiple accounts. You configure a monitoring account and source accounts. Source accounts share their CloudWatch data (metrics, logs, traces) with the monitoring account. The monitoring account can then create dashboards, alarms, and queries across all source account data.
Option1: Configure CloudWatch cross-account observability with a central monitoring account
Option2: Export metrics from each account to S3 and aggregate manually
Option3: Create identical dashboards in each account
Option4: Use AWS Organizations to merge CloudWatch data

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to create a metric filter that counts the number of 404 errors in their application's CloudWatch Logs. Which feature should they use?
Explanation: CloudWatch Logs Metric Filters define patterns to search for in log data and create CloudWatch metrics based on matches. A metric filter with the pattern '404' or a more specific pattern like '[ip, id, user, timestamp, request, status_code=404, size]' counts occurrences and publishes them as a custom metric. This metric can then be used in alarms and dashboards.
Option1: Create a CloudWatch Logs Metric Filter that matches 404 error patterns and publishes a custom metric
Option2: Use CloudWatch Logs Insights to count 404 errors manually
Option3: Parse logs with a Lambda function and publish metrics
Option4: Use AWS X-Ray to track 404 errors

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to monitor the disk space utilization on EC2 instances. This metric is not available in default CloudWatch metrics. Which TWO steps are required? (Select TWO)
Explanation: The CloudWatch Agent must be installed on EC2 instances to collect OS-level metrics like disk space utilization. The agent configuration file (amazon-cloudwatch-agent.json) must include the disk metrics section specifying which filesystems and mount points to monitor. Once configured, disk metrics appear as custom metrics in CloudWatch and can be used in alarms and dashboards.
Option1: Install the CloudWatch Agent on the EC2 instances
Option2: Configure the agent to collect disk utilization metrics in the agent configuration file
Option3: Enable detailed monitoring on the EC2 instances
Option4: Use EC2 instance metadata to retrieve disk metrics

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to set up an automated response that creates a snapshot of an EBS volume when a CloudWatch Alarm triggers. Which approach requires the LEAST custom code?
Explanation: CloudWatch Alarms can trigger Systems Manager Automation documents directly. The AWS-CreateSnapshot automation document creates an EBS snapshot when invoked. By configuring the CloudWatch Alarm to trigger this SSM Automation document, the administrator gets automated snapshot creation without writing any custom Lambda code. SSM Automation handles the API calls and error handling.
Option1: Configure the CloudWatch Alarm to trigger the AWS-CreateSnapshot Systems Manager Automation document
Option2: Write a Lambda function to create snapshots when triggered by the alarm
Option3: Use a cron job on the instance to create snapshots
Option4: Manually create snapshots when alerted

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to ensure that their Auto Scaling group launches instances using the latest patched AMI without manual intervention. Which approach automates this?
Explanation: Systems Manager Automation can be used with EC2 Image Builder to automatically create new AMIs with the latest patches. A Lambda function (or SSM Automation) then updates the Auto Scaling group's launch template with the new AMI ID. When instances are replaced during scaling events or rolling updates, they use the latest patched AMI automatically.
Option1: Use EC2 Image Builder to create patched AMIs and automatically update the Auto Scaling group launch template
Option2: Manually update the AMI ID in the launch template after each patch cycle
Option3: Use User Data scripts to patch instances at launch
Option4: SSH into running instances to apply patches

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to implement a solution where an RDS database is automatically restored from a snapshot if the primary instance becomes corrupted. Which approach should they use?
Explanation: AWS Backup provides automated backup and restore capabilities. Combined with CloudWatch Alarms that detect database corruption indicators (like high error rates or failed health checks) and Lambda functions that trigger a restore from the latest backup, this provides automated recovery. AWS Backup supports cross-Region copies for additional protection.
Option1: Configure AWS Backup with automated restore triggered by CloudWatch Alarms and Lambda
Option2: Manually restore from a snapshot when corruption is detected
Option3: Use Multi-AZ which automatically handles corruption
Option4: Rely on RDS automated backups without any automation

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to test whether their application can handle the failure of a specific AWS service dependency. Which approach should they use?
Explanation: AWS Fault Injection Simulator (FIS) is a managed service for running fault injection experiments. FIS can simulate failures like stopping EC2 instances, throttling API calls, injecting latency, and disrupting network connectivity. Running controlled experiments helps identify weaknesses in the application's resilience before real failures occur.
Option1: Use AWS Fault Injection Simulator to simulate service failures in a controlled manner
Option2: Manually terminate instances in production to test resilience
Option3: Review architecture diagrams without actual testing
Option4: Wait for a real failure to test the application's response

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to ensure that their EFS file system remains available if an AZ fails. Which EFS storage class provides multi-AZ durability by default?
Explanation: EFS Standard storage class automatically replicates data across multiple Availability Zones within a Region, providing high availability and durability. If an AZ becomes unavailable, the file system remains accessible from mount targets in other AZs. EFS One Zone storage class stores data in a single AZ and is less expensive but does not survive AZ failures.
Option1: Use EFS Standard storage class which replicates across multiple AZs
Option2: Use EFS One Zone storage class with manual backups
Option3: Create separate EFS file systems in each AZ
Option4: Use EBS volumes instead of EFS for multi-AZ durability

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to detect when a CloudFormation stack's actual resource configuration has drifted from the template definition. Which feature should they use?
Explanation: CloudFormation Drift Detection compares the current state of stack resources with the expected state defined in the template. It identifies resources that have been modified outside of CloudFormation (manual changes via console, CLI, or other tools). Drift detection shows exactly which properties have changed and their expected vs. actual values.
Option1: Use CloudFormation Drift Detection to identify resources that have been modified outside the template
Option2: Compare the template with the current resources manually
Option3: Use AWS Config to detect CloudFormation drift
Option4: Redeploy the stack to fix any drift

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to automate the process of creating and managing maintenance windows for patching EC2 instances. Which Systems Manager feature should they use?
Explanation: Systems Manager Maintenance Windows define a schedule for performing potentially disruptive actions on instances. You specify the schedule (cron/rate expression), duration, targets (by tag or instance ID), and tasks (Run Command, Automation, Lambda, Step Functions). Maintenance Windows ensure patches are applied during approved time periods, reducing the risk of unplanned downtime.
Option1: Configure Systems Manager Maintenance Windows with patch tasks
Option2: Use CloudWatch Events to trigger patching at specific times
Option3: Create a cron job on each instance for self-patching
Option4: Manually patch instances during off-hours

QuestionText: A SysOps administrator needs to ensure that new EC2 instances launched by an Auto Scaling group automatically register with Systems Manager and have specific software installed. Which TWO approaches should they use? (Select TWO)
Explanation: An IAM instance profile with the AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore policy ensures instances automatically register with Systems Manager when launched. User Data scripts (or SSM State Manager associations) can install required software during instance initialization. Together they ensure every new instance is managed by SSM and has the required software from the moment it launches.
Option1: Attach an IAM instance profile with AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore to the launch template
Option2: Use User Data or SSM State Manager associations to install required software at launch
Option3: Manually register each new instance with Systems Manager
Option4: Install software by SSH after the instance launches
